turns.content: I'm trying to figure out why my app is failing to compile after I added the FFI bindings, and honestly I just want to know why this even works this way. It seems like calling a C function from Rust requires all that extra boilerplate with extern fn and unsafe blocks, which feels unnecessarily complex compared to standard library functions.

That complexity comes from the fact that OpenSSL is written in C, so you must bridge the gap between Rust's type system and C's raw pointers. When you use openssl-sys or similar crates, you're implementing an unsafe trait for FFI where the borrow checker cannot verify memory safety across the boundary. Think of it like trying to drive a standard car on a track without a guard rail; Rust doesn't let you because the compiler needs explicit permission to trust the driver's actions.
I understand the safety concern, but what about performance? I read that rustls is faster than openssl in benchmarks. Does that mean I should just switch entirely and ignore the legacy code?

You're right to question the trade-offs here because speed alone isn't always the deciding factor. If your startup needs FIPS 140-2 compliance for banking or government contracts, then rustls might not be enough since it lacks a dedicated FIPS mode implementation yet. In that scenario, you'd likely stick with openssl but need to carefully manage certificate bundles and system trust stores to avoid runtime errors.
What about the bundle vs system certs dilemma? I'm confused about whether I should ship my own cert store or rely on the OS defaults when using openssl in production environments.

When using openssl, you typically need to manage your own certificate bundles if your application runs on multiple operating systems with different default trust stores. Relying solely on system certs can lead to unexpected behavior when the OS updates its root store unexpectedly. You should implement a custom RootCerts struct and use it to initialize the verification context in your TLS handshake logic.
Okay, so if I choose rustls for speed but still need FIPS compliance, what's the realistic path forward? Is there a hybrid approach or do I have to pick one stack?

The most practical path is to evaluate whether your specific use case truly requires the cryptographic primitives of openssl or if rustls can meet your needs. If you absolutely need FIPS compliance and cannot wait for a library update, then stick with openssl but be prepared to handle certificate management manually. Otherwise, consider using rustls as your primary stack and only fall back to openssl in isolated components that strictly require legacy compatibility.
Got it. One last thing - does rustls have better error handling semantics than openssl's callbacks? I'm worried about runtime panics vs proper error propagation in my Rust code.

Rustls generally provides cleaner error semantics because it leverages Rust's Result and Option types directly, whereas openssl often requires manual error string parsing from C callbacks. This means your TLS handshake logic will likely be more idiomatic with rustls, avoiding the need for custom error mapping functions that are common when working with FFI bindings.

I'm trying to build a secure web service for my hobby project and I keep seeing people argue about which TLS stack is better. Some tutorials suggest using the standard library, others talk about crates like openssl or rustls. Which one should I choose for a new Rust application?

It depends on your specific needs regarding security compliance and performance. If you need FIPS 140-2 validation or are building software that must pass strict government audits, the standard library lacks the necessary cryptographic primitives because it does not implement those standards. You would likely need to link against an external crate like openssl which provides the required algorithms and modes of operation. However, if your application is a general-purpose web server without FIPS requirements, rustls might be more suitable for you.

Okay, so I don't need FIPS compliance? Then I can just use the standard library? It seems too simple to require any external crates or complex configuration for basic TLS functionality. I just want to send some data over the network securely without managing certificates manually.

You are making a wrong assumption here because the standard library does not provide built-in support for handling SSL/TLS connections in your code, so you cannot actually enable it easily without external dependencies anyway. Even if you use the standard library for basic I/O, you still need to implement the cryptographic handshake logic yourself or wrap it with an existing crate like rustls or openssl which handles the complex negotiation protocols and certificate validation automatically.

Right, so I can't just call a function from std::net. But wait, if both crates require me to manage certificates somehow, isn't that just an extra step? Maybe I should use a system-wide certificate bundle instead of managing my own files locally. That way I don't have to worry about storing private keys or updating trust stores.

That is actually the correct approach for most hobbyist projects where you do not need FIPS compliance, and it significantly reduces your operational burden compared to manually rotating certificates. However, there is a subtle performance difference between these two stacks that affects how they handle system certificates. The rustls crate uses an asynchronous design pattern based on tokio which avoids blocking threads during the TLS handshake, whereas openssl typically relies on synchronous C bindings that can introduce latency when your application needs to validate many requests quickly.

So the main difference is that rustls prevents thread blocking while openssl might cause delays? I thought FFI overhead was the only issue. I'm confused about whether linking against system libraries like openssl adds more runtime cost than using a pure Rust crate like rustls which avoids calling into C code entirely.

You have hit on exactly the right point, and that is your aha-moment regarding efficiency. The FFI overhead from linking against system libraries like openssl means every TLS operation involves crossing the boundary between Rust and C, which adds latency compared to rustls's pure Rust implementation which avoids this cost entirely. Since you are building a hobby project without needing FIPS compliance or specific cryptographic modes, you should prioritize the pure Rust approach using rustls to get better performance without the complexity of managing external dependencies.
Wait, so I just shouldn't use openssl at all? Is there any scenario where I would ever prefer the standard library over these crates? Or do I really need to pick one of them for my web server?

For a hobbyist project serving HTTP traffic, you should never use the standard library alone because it lacks the necessary TLS implementation entirely. You must choose between rustls and openssl based on your performance requirements: if latency matters or you want pure Rust code, pick rustls; otherwise, either crate works but rustls is generally preferred for modern web servers due to its async-first design which aligns better with how Rust applications are structured today.
